bibliographic.citation: King Horn: A Middle-English Romance, Edited from the Manuscripts, ed. Joseph Hall (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1901).
bibliographic.title: King Horn: A Middle-English Romance, Edited from the Manuscripts

entities.description: The immediate print source. Hall prints close transcriptions of all three King Horn witnesses in parallel, expands contractions in italics, corrects only what he regards as obvious scribal errors while normally reporting manuscript readings, punctuates the Cambridge text, and adds introduction, commentary, glossary, indexes, and the distinct Horn Childe appendix.
entities.label: Hall's 1901 King Horn

rights.basis: The edition was first published in the United Kingdom in 1901. Even if it qualified for URAA restoration, restored protection lasts only for the remainder of the term it would otherwise have had; the applicable maximum pre-1978 term has expired.
rights.component: 1901 published edition text

entities.description: The unique witness of Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, ff. 317va-323vb, copied by Auchinleck Scribe 1. The 1,136 surviving lines are arranged in twelve-line tail-rhyme stanzas; a leaf is lost after fol. 321 and the ending is imperfect after fol. 323.

holding.notes: The Faculty of Advocates shelfmark changed in 1840; the manuscript passed to the National Library of Scotland in 1925. Horn Childe occupies ff. 317va-323vb.

next_steps: Ask CUDL for clarification before metadata redistribution that depends on the version.

next_steps: Treat the works and witnesses as related without directional filiation.
Revisit only with dedicated textual-critical evidence.
notes: Hall's historical stemmatic and source suggestions should not be converted into directional lineage without stronger evidence.
openQuestions.question: Can a secure textual genealogy among the three King Horn witnesses or among King Horn, Horn et Rimenhild, Horn Childe, and Hind Horn be established from current scholarship?

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is the anonymous Middle English romance King Horn, not Hall's whole 1901 volume and not one particular manuscript version.
Harley, Laud, and Cambridge are parallel source witnesses. Their line totals and textual differences must not be collapsed into one notional canonical transcription.
Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild is a separate romance printed and encoded as an appendix; it is related to King Horn but is not a work part or a fourth witness of King Horn.
Hall's manuscript genealogy and dialect localizations are preserved as historical editorial hypotheses, not substituted for current institutional cataloging or asserted as secure filiation.

The complete public Digital Bodleian and Cambridge Digital Library objects are cached as image sets. King Horn occupies Bodleian IIIF canvases 454-471 and CUDL canvases 15-29. The British Library describes a complete digitization of Harley MS 2253, but its images remain unavailable during service recovery.

The U-M XML's 396 PB instances refer to 352 unique page images: 44 references are duplicated because Hall's Harley and Laud columns share printed pages before the encoding serializes them into separate divisions.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a U-M 2006 uncorrected-OCR encoding derived from Joseph Hall's 1901 Clarendon edition. Hall prints three separate close manuscript transcriptions of King Horn in parallel: British Library Harley MS 2253, Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 108, and Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(2). The XML serializes those print columns as consecutive complete versions, then encodes Hall's appendix Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, a distinct romance transcribed chiefly from the Auchinleck manuscript with a small number of readings supplied or corrected from earlier editors. This graph distinguishes the abstract King Horn work, its three witnesses, Hall's edition, U-M's encoding, the repository artifact, the separate Horn Childe work and witness, prior editions Hall consulted, and later comparison scholarship.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Probably later thirteenth century; traditionally dated c. 1225, with the 1270s proposed as a plausible estimate
catalogSummary.region: Southern or southeastern England; exact place of composition disputed
catalogSummary.title: King Horn

catalogingSubject.label: King Horn
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English romance King Horn as an abstract work represented by three substantially variant complete manuscript texts. This subject excludes the individual witnesses, Joseph Hall's 1901 parallel-text edition, U-M's OCR encoding, and the repository XML. It also excludes Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, a distinct treatment of the Horn story that Hall prints as an appendix and U-M encodes in full.

assertion.notes: The normalized span follows PCMEP's broad evidential range; it does not imply composition throughout those years.

dateStatements.display: Probably the later thirteenth century; the older conventional date is c. 1225, while the 1270s have been proposed as a plausible estimate
notes: The dates of the three surviving manuscripts are later and must not be substituted for the work's composition date.

extent.description: One continuous verse romance with no universal line count assigned. The repository encoding has 1,546 L elements for Harley 2253, 1,569 for Laud Misc. 108, and 1,530 for Gg.4.27(2). These are separate complete witness texts, not parts to be added; the XML's further 1,138 L elements belong to the distinct Horn Childe appendix and include two ellipsis placeholders for lacunae.

formStatements.description: Short-line narrative verse in rhyming couplets, usually with three stresses and substantial alliterative patterning, though metre and lineation vary. Harley 2253 writes two printed verses on many manuscript long lines.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The repository header's generic 'eng' code is superseded here by direct identification of the medieval language as Middle English. Modern English, French, Latin, and German occur only in editorial or scholarly layers.
The XML header declares only 'eng'; direct inspection and scholarship establish Middle English as the work language.
The 5,783 L elements in the XML are not a work-level extent: they sum three complete variant texts of King Horn and the separate Horn Childe appendix.
Hall's manuscript genealogy and specific original-dialect localization are historical scholarly hypotheses, not unqualified facts.
Witness dates, localizations, layouts, and image rights remain attached to their exact manuscript or facsimile layers in the lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult Rosamund Allen's 1984 edition and 1988 date-and-provenance study directly.
Compare current linguistic profiles for all three witnesses and distinguish rhyme evidence from scribal spelling.
openQuestions.question: Can the broad later-thirteenth-century date and southern or southeastern localization of King Horn be refined without treating one witness's scribal dialect as the work's original language?

next_steps: Review modern comparative work after Hall, including Allen and Mills, before replacing related_to with a directional relation.
Keep shared story material distinct from evidence of direct textual copying.
openQuestions.question: What, if any, direct textual relationship can current scholarship establish among King Horn, Horn et Rimenhild, Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, and the Hind Horn ballad tradition?

next_steps: Compare a modern synoptic edition and manuscript lineation, especially Harley's two-verses-per-written-line layout.
Until then retain witness-specific line counts and content_structure_status=summary.
openQuestions.question: Is there a defensible canonical lineation or work-level internal structure that can be mapped across all three complete King Horn witnesses?

notes: The witnesses have distinct scribal dialects and do not preserve one unchanged authorial dialect.

scope.description: Reconstructed language of the work, not any one scribal witness

assertion.notes: Hall proposed northwestern Surrey; later work has found the phonology compatible with London, but neither location is secure.

placeStatements.placeLabel: Southeastern England outside Kent, near the Midland border

notes: This is a narrative setting, not evidence for the place of composition.

scope.description: Selected narrative episodes involving Horn's second exile, service, combat, and alliance; no stable content-part structure is asserted

notes: The three surviving scribes and the modern editors are not authors of the anonymous cross-witness work.

scope.description: Separate close transcriptions of the Harley, Laud, and Cambridge texts
Parallel alignment, comparison, and manuscript-relationship analysis

subjects.label: Christian-Saracen conflict and conversion
notes: The label describes the romance's medieval antagonistic religious framework; it is not a modern endorsement of that representation.

summaries.value: After Saracen invaders kill King Murry and seize Suddene, his son Horn and twelve companions are set adrift and reach Westernesse. Horn grows into a knight and pledges himself to Rymenhild, but the traitor Fikenhild falsely accuses him and causes a second exile. In Ireland Horn defeats a Saracen champion and gathers allies; he returns in disguise to prevent Rymenhild's forced marriage, reconquers Suddene, restores Christian rule and his inheritance, and finally rescues Rymenhild from Fikenhild. The romance joins love and recognition with exile, loyal companionship, betrayal, martial testing, and legitimate political restoration.

This record describes the anonymous medieval romance across its three surviving witnesses. Hall's edition, the U-M encoding, and the repository artifact are separate lineage layers. Horn Childe and Maiden Rimnild, although fully encoded after King Horn in the same XML, is a distinct related romance and is not a part of this work.

King Horn survives complete in British Library Harley MS 2253, Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 108, and Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(2). Hall prints the three texts in parallel rather than constructing one unified eclectic poem; U-M serializes those parallel columns into three consecutive version divisions. No stable cross-witness internal part structure is imposed here.

notes: The spelling 'Geste of Kyng Horn' appears as a witness title in the Harley catalog; the Cambridge digital catalog uses 'The Geste of King Horn'.

work.summary: After Saracen invaders kill King Murry and seize Suddene, his son Horn and twelve companions are set adrift and reach Westernesse. Horn grows into a knight and pledges himself to Rymenhild, but the traitor Fikenhild falsely accuses him and causes a second exile. In Ireland Horn defeats a Saracen champion and gathers allies; he returns in disguise to prevent Rymenhild's forced marriage, reconquers Suddene, restores Christian rule and his inheritance, and finally rescues Rymenhild from Fikenhild. The romance joins love and recognition with exile, loyal companionship, betrayal, martial testing, and legitimate political restoration.
